evidence: Academies have their local towns, regions, catchment areas locked the fuck down. And that is why you can reliably generate talent. If you aren't able to do that, you don't survive as an academy.
Ivor immediately tracked ('yeah, 100%') when V articulated territorial lock-in as the future of work

Completely off the cuff. Here's what I would be thinking about. I would be thinking about trying to capture every college graduate from today [...] as their career coach. Note taker. Not taker, but like notebook, if you like.

The value they get in the first six months will be like... yeah. The value they'll have in six years time if they keep up with it will be wild. Those people won't just be users, they'll be like fans and advocates.

I've become so black pilled on the idea that we are so limited by the ATS and the realities and the practical limits of how we process talent at scale that it's hard to even conceive of a world where you could have actual insight into how someone thinks before talking to them.

Honestly kind of my ideal scenario is just getting picked up by a company that has the wisdom to realize we will look like absolutely look like the good guys if we just give Careerspan for free to every college kid in the country.

source_display: The Quiet Part Problem as Market Opportunity
evidence: Employers have selection criteria they cannot legally state. The gap between stated job requirements and actual selection preferences is a structural feature of hiring markets, not a bug.

source_display: Response Rate as Primary Success Metric
evidence: Logan's insight: use response rates as the convincing proxy — 'our users pay attention to our recommendations'

I wouldn't think of it as a safety blanket. I would think of it as demonstrating—our demonstrating our worth upfront. [...] what it does at the outset is it gets these recruiters that have been burned by software and that are not super trusting of AI to see that it does, in fact, generate some names worth paying attention to.

An Indian recruiter placing US engineers said: 'I have nothing to give these people besides the resume. All of these people look the same.' The commoditization already happened—AI just made it visible.
Superposition wouldn't bring users. They might not even bring significant revenue. But their logo creates a trust cascade. This is pure signaling economics—the brand IS the product.
Everything you just said about Superposition is also true about working with a recruiter specifically. Except we don't get to put Superposition on our website.

V's counter to 'you only have 500 users' was essentially: 'Yes, but they're the right 500.' This inverts the network effects playbook. Instead of 'we're valuable because we're big,' it's 'we're valuable because we're exclusive.'
When you tell them yes, but we can make sure that it's all top 20 users from their alumni associations... Literally no one in the world can offer that.
Logan's biotech friend disrupted her industry by going directly to morgues instead of through middlemen. 'My middleman is charging me too much money' → go direct to source.
